_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Testing practice 1
questions.prompt: Which input is the best boundary case for a function that finds the largest list item?
choices: A list with one item
A list with ten random items
The same three-item list twice
A list already used in the demo
questions.explanation: One item is the smallest valid list for this function. It checks whether the code wrongly assumes that a second item exists.

questions.title: Testing practice 2
questions.prompt: What happens when an assert expression is false?
choices: Python raises AssertionError
Python changes 5 to 4
The line is ignored
Python returns False from the program
questions.explanation: An assert statement stops at a false condition and raises AssertionError. Test tools catch that error and show which expectation failed.

questions.title: Trace Testing
questions.code:
def is_even(number):
    return number % 2 == 0

assert is_even(4) is True
assert is_even(7) is False
print("checks passed")
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: Each assertion states one expectation. If an expression is false, Python raises AssertionError and points to the failed check. The resulting output is 'checks passed'.

questions.title: Testing good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Testing?
choices: Test one clear behavior at a time.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Test one clear behavior at a time. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Testing practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices: assert total([2, 3, 5]) == 10
assert total(nums) == sum(x for x in nums)
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: A test should check a result, not repeat the function's calculation in a more complicated form. Direct expected values are easier to review.

questions.title: Testing practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'A passing test can still be weak'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
A test proves only the case it checks. If every test uses positive numbers, a bug involving zero or negative values can remain hidden.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: A test proves only the case it checks. If every test uses positive numbers, a bug involving zero or negative values can remain hidden.

questions.title: Testing practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Testing?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Use fixed inputs with obvious expected results.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use fixed inputs with obvious expected results. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Testing practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Testing?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Include normal, boundary, and unusual cases.
questions.explanation: Include normal, boundary, and unusual cases. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Testing practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Testing?
choices: Give tests names that describe the behavior.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Give tests names that describe the behavior. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Testing practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Testing?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Run the tests again after every meaningful change.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Run the tests again after every meaningful change. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
